text.title: E2E Framework Migration — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that prevent multi-week migration projects from stalling forever, dual-running forever, or shipping broken cutovers.

rule.statement: Before flag-flip (Wave 3), the team MUST demonstrate functional parity: every customer-visible behavior covered by an existing test passes equivalently on the new framework. Parity gate fails if any test passes on old AND fails on new, with no documented justification (and ticket to fix).

rationale.source: Strangler-fig literature, large-scale migration case studies (Etsy, Stripe, Shopify)

rule.rationale: Skipping parity creates "the new system mostly works" surprises in production — the most expensive class of migration defect because rollback is harder once cutover commits. Parity gate is the cheap insurance.

rule.statement: For mechanical transformations (API rename, import-path change, syntax shift), an automated codemod MUST be written and applied. Manual conversion of mechanical changes is forbidden — it introduces inconsistency and slows the migration. Codemod scope is documented; non-mechanical changes (semantic differences) get human review per module.

rationale.source: Facebook jscodeshift case studies, Vue Migration Build patterns

rule.rationale: Manual mechanical changes have ~5% defect rate even with senior engineers; codemods have <0.5% on the mechanical portion. Codemods also force the team to articulate which changes are mechanical vs semantic, which reveals migration risk early.

rule.statement: Wave 2 (parallel build) MUST include a documented dual-write OR dual-test window: either both frameworks run in parallel (dual-write for stateful), or both test suites run in CI (dual-test for tests), for a defined duration (typically 1-3 weeks). The window has an explicit end date — forbidden: indefinite dual-running.

rationale.source: Dual-write migration patterns (database / framework), continuous-delivery research

rule.rationale: Dual-running is a safety net during parallel build but becomes tech debt if perpetual. Explicit end date forces commitment to cutover and prevents the "we'll just keep both" stall pattern that kills many migrations at 70% complete.

rule.statement: Each wave MUST have a documented rollback path that can be executed within a defined RTO (typically <= 30 min). The rollback path is exercised at least once before the next wave begins (rollback fire drill). Wave entry is blocked until prior-wave rollback is rehearsed.
rule.rationale: Untested rollback paths fail in actual emergencies. Rehearsal turns "we can roll back" from hope into a tested capability. The 30-min RTO keeps the rollback realistic — long rollbacks are not actually rollbacks; they're new outages.

rule.statement: Wave 4 (decommission) MUST be executed. The old framework / language / library is removed from the codebase, feature flags retired, related CI jobs deleted, documentation updated to reference only the new system. Migrations that skip Wave 4 are NOT complete — they leave dual-system tech debt that compounds future migrations.

rationale.source: Tech-debt accumulation research, organizational learning on stalled migrations

rule.rationale: Empirically, migrations that skip decommission produce 2-3x harder NEXT migrations because the codebase now has both old AND new frameworks indefinitely. Forcing Wave 4 prevents this compound debt.

example.title: Wave-2 module port with codemod + parity test
example.code:
Migration: Cypress -> Playwright (3-month plan) Wave: 2 (parallel build), Module: checkout-flow
Inventory: - 18 Cypress tests in tests/e2e/checkout/*.cy.ts - 4 use cy.intercept (mechanical port via codemod) - 12 use cy.get + chai assertions (mechanical port + assertion-style change via codemod) - 2 use cy.task (semantic — needs human review, no codemod)
Codemod authored: scripts/cypress-to-playwright-codemod.ts Handles: cy.get -> page.locator, cy.intercept -> page.route, chai -> expect, cy.visit -> page.goto.
Applied to: 16 mechanical tests. Code review confirmed no semantic drift. Manual port: 2 cy.task tests; review by checkout-feature owner.
Parity test: Ran Cypress and Playwright suites against the same staging deploy. Result: 17/18 pass on both. 1 difference: Playwright detected a real timing bug Cypress was masking. Filed ticket TICKET-2340, documented as a discovered issue (not a migration defect).
Wave entry/exit: Entry criteria met: ADR-2026-005 approved, Wave 1 PoC passed parity. Exit criteria for this module: codemod applied + parity pass + PR merged + rollback documented (revert PR ready in branch).
Status: module-DONE; 8 of 12 checkout-flow modules complete. Dual-test window: ends 2026-06-15 (2 weeks from now).
example.note: Illustrates r1-r4: parity gate, codemod for mechanical, dual-window with end date, rollback ready.

example.title: Parallel-run-forever stall
example.code:
Migration: Selenium -> Playwright Started: 12 months ago Current state: - 60% of tests on Playwright - 40% still on Selenium - CI runs BOTH suites on every PR (3x build time) - "We'll finish next quarter" -- has been said 4 times - No flag-flip date. No decommission plan. No Wave 4.
Outcome: developers double-maintain tests, both suites get stale, flaky tests accumulate, team morale degrades, migration never closes.
Repair: enforce r3 + r5. Set hard end date for dual-window. After end date, remaining Selenium tests are converted in a 2-week sprint OR retired with explicit rationale. Wave 4 happens.
example.note: Illustrates r3, r5 violations — the most common migration failure: parallel-running forever.
